A system is built to infer how a discovery was made by extracting object-concepts from a journal and then searching over recombinations of those concepts. If the extraction reflects the system’s own preferred decomposition rather than the real causal structure Einstein used, then the search space is already biased at its foundation. What appears to be a reconstruction of a historical reasoning process is instead a search through the model’s ontology.

The proposal assumes tractability comes from narrowing. That works only if the relevant path to discovery lies inside the narrowed region. But if some discoveries require first widening into bizarre ontologies and only later narrowing, then premature narrowing excludes the very trajectories that matter. In that case, the system will preferentially recover discoveries that are legible to its initial decomposition and miss those that required conceptual detours alien to it.

Now add recursive recycling. Outputs from one round are fed back into the search pool as candidate advances for later rounds. If some of those outputs are bad but internally coherent, they become dangerous. Because they cohere, they survive filtering. Because they are recycled, they accumulate influence. Search-pool composition then becomes an edge case of central importance: a pool seeded with polished mistakes can drift toward a self-supporting pseudo-lineage of progress.

Under these premises, the strange strings matter not as interpretable theories but as examples of coherent-seeming artifacts that need not correspond to genuine causal structure: “Medicaids barbedness nonrudimentariness isotimic relics.” “Unclosed arthra nanosomus agitator socializer.” They are token sequences that can enter a pool, remain internally pattern-like, and be treated as material for further decomposition. A model biased toward its own ontology may still parse them into reusable units. A narrowing-first procedure may prune away unconventional but fruitful alternatives while retaining these because they fit local scoring criteria. Recursive recycling then lets them function as ancestors of later outputs.

The result is a system that becomes more tractable and more wrong at once. It searches efficiently, but through a world partly made of its own decomposition and partly populated by coherent debris from earlier rounds. Because widening is underused, there is no robust mechanism for escaping that basin. Because recycling is allowed, internally coherent error can imitate cumulative insight. So the implied world is one where apparent progress no longer reliably tracks discovery. It tracks compatibility with the model’s ontology, the narrowing heuristic, and the current composition of the pool.
